Thank you.

I will set the scene for you a little bit.
We're hiring right now for this technical managing editor role.
It fits into the organization at a layer where we want some kind of...
Awareness of workflows and of using AI to accomplish any task, but obviously content is what we're aimed at currently.
And utilizing agentic workflows, utilizing AI tooling, and utilizing just general standard workflows to create client work.
The role is somewhat client-facing, interfacing with clients on a tactical level, understanding and reporting on content performance, that sort of thing, guiding the overall strategy.
And then working internally with content managers.
The way that we view the architecture is the technical managing editor is there to like clear blockers for the CMs, architect the pipeline, not build it.
You don't have to build it, we have engineers for that, but to understand what it's missing and what it needs to deliver good client work, and mainly about inputs and outputs.
Like what inputs are going in?
What outputs are coming out?
Where can we tweak that?
And then help the content managers, clear blockers, because we really view the pipelines themselves as the writers.
So these pipelines are creating content.
They have humans in the loop.
Those are the content managers.

And then the managing editor is there to help those content managers, clear blockers, check their work, assign tasks, kind of understand where the pipelines are operating optimally and where they're not.
Because the content managers may not have the little bit, you know, step back view of, okay, how does this fit in with overall client goals?
And what has the client been telling me?
And, you know, you're sort of reading their intent and understanding that and going, okay, I think we were working on, we have internal solutions for that and we can achieve that.
So that's kind of like the interface.
So just a little scene setting, and I'd love to hear a little bit about you in that context.
time.
to me about work that you've done previously with workflows, with AI tooling, with content, just to get a little bit of an idea of kind of what you've done before.

So I'll just talk about, say, since the dawn of ChatGPT.
So early 2022, late 2022 is when I joined this company called Alexa Translations.
So part of my role was figuring out, and this is just before ChatGPT came onto the scene, right?
And this company, they used to do legal translations for clients like KPMG, Deloitte, so very detail-oriented clients.
And my job was to set up translation workflows so they had content that needed to be translated.
And my team was a bunch of content writers, specifically.
didn't have content managers, so I was doing, I was wearing multiple hats at the time.

So part of it was interfacing with clients, figuring out what their wants were, because in many cases, what I've seen is clients are not really direct, but you can infer what they need, and then figuring out how we could develop.
That was part of it.
And then I joined this company, Directive.
So Directive was, it's an agency, it's a B2B SaaS agency, and they went hard on AI content production.
So even though my title was senior SEO strategist, all content that was produced, all SEO reporting, all content reporting was going through me.
So every week we had stand-ups with clients, like bi-weekly or sometimes weekly stand-ups.
Clients would come in and say, hey, our CMO demands this, our CRO wants this.

Tell us about how certain content is performing, why are lead magnets not being downloaded, how are we supporting bottom of the file content or top of the file content, what keywords are we going after.
quarterly setting up the strategy and then working with a team in-house.
we had this upsell that we would offer like content production, which is, I believe, what GrowthX does.
And we had two layers.
One was human-oriented content, so fully thought leadership-focused content written by experts.
And then another was human plus AI.
And
So a part of it was educating the client about how these two differ and what they can expect to gain from it.
And then part two was figuring out the blockers or I would say the obstacles that clients had in their head.
Because again, education around AI-focused content was scant to say the least.
And then talking to them one-on-one, managing their internal client team.
So directive, they used Asana.
So setting up client dashboards, making sure the client had visibility to what work was being done.

So going all the way to the very tactical steps, like from research to setting up workflows to building out content templates, making sure we have buy-in from every member and then executing on those.
And then the last step was setting up dashboards.
So I personally use Looker, but some clients have, let's say, privacy requirements.
So they prefer sticking to the Microsoft ecosystem.
Power BI is one.
So setting up dashboards and then making sure

or that reporting is being done on those client pieces was critically important because one of the things that I saw at least from 2023 to even early this year is there was a lot of focus on bottom line revenues.
How is the content that we are producing actually turning into conversions?
What are we doing to support our marketing campaigns?
So if we have a new product launch, how are we managing that?
So part of it was also optimizing the content.

So that added another layer of CRO, which I can give you suggestions, but you need a human mind to actually anticipate and understand, hey, how are people actually going through this page and what we can do around it?
And then when you're working with content writers, right, you can't expect them to know it all.
So what I would do is we would host like a biweekly lunch and learn sort of thing.
And then just talk about how the team, and this is more from a managerial standpoint, is how they're leveraging.
How they can do better.

This helped, personally, sorry, if I'm going on a tangent here, but this helped create like feedback loops and also helped the team a lot, especially specialists on the team, understand how different AI tools can help them optimize their workflows.

So the task of the ME and one of the reasons that this is such a hard world to hire for is like, it definitely is kind of set, straddles that line between, okay, you need to understand just content tactics and, you know, performance and make, you know, organic, authentic, informed suggestions to a client because a lot of times they are leaning on us.
Now, sometimes we work with larger clients and maybe they have an internal growth team or as you experience, sometimes the SEO person.
And so when we interact with them, sometimes we just have to be the adult in the room about the content and say like, hey, we really, we're seeing these signals.
We're data backed, but also our experience backed.
And we'd like to like move into this cluster of content or develop a new cluster or talk about a new strategy or a reset or whatever.
And like going into those meetings and talking to them on a weekly basis.
And then in between, making sure that we're not just sort of like churning out our deliverables and that they actually are driving towards goals.
Because in the near term, it feels good to deliver on time.
But then six months or 12 year renewal cycles, all of a sudden you're like, what have we done?
What have you done for me over the, you know, what have you done for me lately?
What can you prove that you've done that you've accomplished?
And I think that that's the thing that the disconnect that we want to solve for, that's like a lot.
A our Emmys maybe hadn't done that work before.
Like they've done the editorial components of it, but not so much the reporting and frankly, like, oh gosh, accountability aspect of it.
You know, like, hey, at the end of the day, they're paying you a lot of money and they really want results.
They are not paying you a lot of money to deliver them content on time.
They are paying you money for outcomes.
And I think that that's kind of like a big thing.
So I'm glad you had some experience there.

And then on the workflow side, which is the other, that's one of the reasons it's so hard is because, okay, we can find content strategists who have done a lot of that work and are really deep in that world.
But then they have little to no experience prompting LLMs or understanding how to engineer context.
And because the context engineering components of this, in our case, the way we work is that we generally create a set of core artifacts.
And then instead of like secondary and tertiary artifacts that relate to the content that we're...
The core artifacts are probably not all that surprising.
It's a company context artifact that basically is like, hey, what is this company?
What does it do?
What are its products?
That sort of thing.
Then it is an audience personas artifact, which allows us to detail what audiences we're writing for, who is this content for.
And then the trickiest to execute is the writing guidelines, rules, guidelines.
Write noise, all of that stuff.
Yeah, exactly.
Exactly, exactly.
And those artifacts, and I'm using those in general terms because sometimes they're split into more than three because of where they need to actually be executed in the pipeline.
But in aggregate, those are the ones that are important to create.
But to create those properly, you do need to have some experience with how LLMs parse things and the semantics that is appropriate for them to read and access.
Just context engineering in general is a good chunk of the job.
next time.
Because that's a force multiplier.

And if it's not, then it can feel like a bare knuckle brawl every week to try to get content out because you're struggling with how the pipeline is interpreting the instructions and so on and so forth.

So we use those, we integrate them.
We have, I've set up API integration for these tools, which creates like an internal, I would say, I don't want to use the word content engine because it's been beaten to that so much.

You're like, hey, let's file a ticket.
Let's get this pipeline updated so that it no longer does those things that are costing you time and efficiency, right?

Because, like, at the end of the day, if we can get it to a place where we run a pipeline, and then it's within five to ten minutes of, like, editable for client view or to pass on to you, and so you can gut check it and make sure that you are comfortable presenting it to the client that week, that's scalable, you know, big time.
But if we are like, hey, we run something, and then it takes us an hour and a half to get this in client-facing shape, you can't deliver 30, 40 pieces of content every week.
It's impossible, right?
Whereas we...
When the pipelines do work well, that's actually very easy, you know, to like deliver 20, 30, 40 pieces of content in a week for usually not just one client, but usually like three or four clients.
It's actually very straightforward to do when the pipelines are operating correctly, when they've been architected correctly.

And then working backwards from there on a process level to understand, hey, what's missing from these pipelines?

And another one question I had is, right now, what is the biggest challenge that the CMs face, which is very time-consuming, in your opinion, and that is...
They can be solved quickly.
there's two big time sucks.
One is briefs and assignment generation.
Basically, like, what is the structure of the article?
How do I generate, like, a solid structure that pleases the client that's going to achieve goals?
A lot of time is eaten up there because we have...
So the earlier versions of our pipelines were really designed to do large-scale, keyword-driven, structure-agnostic generation.
In other words, give us 250 keywords.
We will find the best format for this article.
We will do SERP analysis.
This is the pipeline saying we.

So the pipeline, it goes out, pulls the SEMrush API, does SERP analysis, finds the highest-ranking articles for that keyword, analyzes the articles, and understands what format they're in, compares those...
It to an internal library of types, like basically structures, and then says, no problem, I know what you need, and then it generates a brief based on that.
That's cool if the client doesn't care at all about the structure of the article, but how many clients have you worked with that don't care at all about what structure the article takes?
No, yeah, and the more stakeholders are on the call, the more they care, because they have to.
They have to justify their job, right?

And so it's like, hey, trust me, this will rank really well for this keyword doesn't really fly, because they're like, oh, no, I want like how-tos, or I want, you know, complete guides, or, you know, right, right.

They work, and this is, honestly, some of this is based on the fact that, like, the original version of the product was designed for Marcel, and Marcel was an overworked CMO who had no backing by leadership, a.k.a.
the CEO, to, like, spend a bunch of money on the content universe, and he's like, I'm...
No, organic content will do well for us.
We could spend money here instead of throwing it into the black void of Facebook ads, and it will pay dividends long down the road, right?
Which, you know, Facebook ads, the moment you stop paying, the moment they stop producing.
With organic content, you can publish it, establish authority, and it can pay dividends for years, right?
And sometimes it doesn't even begin to pay dividends for months.
But then when it does, you're like, oh, my God, I'm so glad I sowed those seeds months ago, right?
So he was looking for a way to do that fast with almost no input from any other human but himself, right?
And that is the original way the tool was built.

really takes me, like, an hour to build a solid brief, you know, to do the research and do all this.

Okay, how do we get it there?
How do we close that gap?

try to do that every 90 days or so or 60 days just to make sure I understand what Averbrae's real painting is like.
